userStories.title: 9.1.14 — Refactor PreparationManager and StatusEffectManager to use Entity.buffs
userStories.description:
Tests: unit only — Migrate internal storage from private maps in managers to the Entity.buffs map added in MFG-50.
Context: MFG-50 added buffs: Map<string, number> to Entity with addStacks/getStacks/clearStacks/clearAll methods and tested that managers CAN use them. But the managers themselves were NOT refactored — they still own private maps internally.
Current state:
- src/systems/PreparationManager.ts owns a private Map<string, Map<PreparationType, number>> keyed by entity ID
- src/systems/StatusEffectManager.ts owns a private Map<string, number> for burn stacks, external to the entity
- Entity.buffs exists but is not used by either manager in production code
- MFG-50 tests demonstrate the desired pattern (manager writes to entity.buffs) but the actual manager code hasn't been updated
Required changes:
1. Refactor src/systems/PreparationManager.ts:- Remove the private stacks map
- Use entity.addStacks(prepType, count) instead of writing to internal map
- Use entity.getStacks(prepType) instead of reading from internal map
- Use entity.clearStacks(prepType) for clearing specific preps
- Use entity.clearAll() for interruption (damage, defensive reaction, unrelated action)
- Retain rule logic: maxStacks enforcement, paired action validation, interruption rules
- The manager needs to receive the Entity instance (or look it up) instead of just an entity ID string
2. Refactor src/systems/StatusEffectManager.ts:- Remove the private burn stacks map
- Use entity.addStacks('burn', count) / entity.getStacks('burn') / entity.clearStacks('burn')
- Retain resolution logic: end-of-round damage, consumption rules, bleed persistence
- The manager needs to receive the Entity instance for reads/writes
3. Verify all existing tests pass:- features/unit/preparation.feature (8+ scenarios)
- features/unit/status-effects.feature (6+ scenarios)
- features/unit/entity-buffs.feature (8 scenarios)
Acceptance Criteria:
- PreparationManager has NO private stacks map — all stack storage goes through Entity.buffs
- StatusEffectManager has NO private burn/bleed maps — all storage goes through Entity.buffs
- Both managers retain their rule enforcement logic (max stacks, interruption, end-of-round resolution)
- All existing preparation tests pass
- All existing status-effects tests pass
- All existing entity-buffs tests pass
- npm run test passes

userStories.title: 9.1.13 — Move defensive reactions from AttackEffect to ActionPipeline
userStories.description:
Tests: unit + integration — Migrate defensive reaction prompting from AttackEffect into the ActionPipeline so all attack-like effects (Attack, Shoot, Cast) share the same reaction flow.
Context: MFG-52 created ActionPipeline.handleDefensiveReaction() but left it as a no-op stub with the comment "actual reaction logic is in AttackEffect". The whole point of MFG-52 was to move this orchestration OUT of effects and INTO the pipeline.
Current state:
- src/combat/ActionPipeline.ts has handleDefensiveReaction() that does nothing (empty async function)
- src/effects/AttackEffect.ts still contains the full defensive reaction logic: prompting the target for guard/evade, applying the reaction, modifying combat stats
- ShootEffect and CastEffect have NO defensive reaction handling at all — meaning ranged and spell attacks never trigger defensive reactions even when they should (melee spells, close-range shots)
- The existing features/unit/defensive-reactions.feature tests pass because they test AttackEffect directly
Required changes:
1. Move the defensive reaction logic from AttackEffect.execute() into ActionPipeline.handleDefensiveReaction():- Accept: context, target entity, attackPower, attackAgility
- If target is player-controlled: prompt for defensive reaction (guard/evade) via context.adapter
- If target is monster: use AI reaction selection (existing logic)
- Return the chosen reaction (type + bead count) so the caller can factor it into combat resolution
- Use buildDefensiveOptions() from AttackResolvers.ts (already extracted in iteration 1)
2. Update AttackEffect.execute() to call ActionPipeline.handleDefensiveReaction() instead of doing it inline
3. Update ShootEffect.execute() to call handleDefensiveReaction() before resolving combat (ranged attacks should allow guard but not evade — adjust options accordingly)
4. Update CastEffect.execute() — spells should allow Ward-based resistance (via MagicalResolver), not guard/evade. Consider whether defensive reactions apply to spells at all per game design.
5. Ensure existing defensive-reactions.feature tests still pass after the migration
Acceptance Criteria:
- ActionPipeline.handleDefensiveReaction() contains the actual prompting and reaction logic (not a stub)
- AttackEffect delegates to the pipeline for defensive reactions
- ShootEffect calls handleDefensiveReaction() before combat resolution
- All existing defensive-reactions.feature scenarios pass
- Minimum 4 new scenarios testing pipeline-level reactions (player prompt, monster auto-react, reaction applied to combat stats, no reaction for non-targetable)
- npm run test passes

userStories.title: 9.1.12 — Wire CastEffect to spell resolution (Intensity vs Ward)
userStories.description:
Tests: unit only — Fix CastEffect to use the spell resolution system instead of physical attack resolution.
Context: MFG-52 created CastEffect as a thin wrapper, but it incorrectly calls resolveAttack() (physical combat: power vs defense/guard/evasion) instead of using the magical resolution path built in MFG-13 (MagicalResolver) and MFG-17 (CastResolver).
Current state (broken):
- src/effects/CastEffect.ts calls resolveAttack({ power: spellPower, agility: spellPower }, defenseStats, [])
- This means spells are resolved as physical attacks — they check guard/evasion instead of Ward
- The Intensity vs Ward gate check from MFG-13 is completely bypassed
- Channel stack cost reduction from MFG-17 is not used
What CastEffect should do:
1. Accept spell definition params: spellColor, baseCost, baseDamage, channelStacks, extraBeads
2. Call CastResolver.resolveCast() to compute effective cost, intensity, and damage
3. Call MagicalResolver.resolveMagicalEffect() for the Intensity vs Ward gate check
4. Use the isAlly parameter to allow ally targets to skip Ward check
5. Return cast-specific events (not attack events)
Required changes:
1. Modify src/effects/CastEffect.ts:- Import CastResolver and MagicalResolver instead of resolveAttack
- Accept spell params: spellColor, baseCost, baseDamage, channelStacks, extraBeads, isAlly
- Call CastResolver.resolveCast({ color, baseCost, baseDamage, channelStacks, extraBeads, targetWard })
- Call MagicalResolver.resolveMagicalEffect({ intensity, ward, isAlly }) for gate check
- Only apply damage if magical effect manifests (intensity > ward OR isAlly accepts)
- Build spell-specific animation events
2. Add/update tests in the existing cast-resolver.feature or create a cast-effect.feature:- Scenario: CastEffect resolves damage via Intensity vs Ward (not power vs defense)
- Scenario: CastEffect blocked when intensity <= ward on enemy
- Scenario: CastEffect hits ally who voluntarily accepts
- Scenario: CastEffect uses channel stacks to reduce bead cost
- Scenario: CastEffect extra beads increase intensity
Acceptance Criteria:
- CastEffect calls CastResolver.resolveCast() + MagicalResolver.resolveMagicalEffect(), NOT resolveAttack()
- Intensity vs Ward gate check is applied for enemy targets
- Ally targets can voluntarily accept (skip Ward check)
- Channel stacks reduce effective spell cost
- Extra beads increase intensity
- Minimum 5 test scenarios for CastEffect specifically
- npm run test passes

userStories.title: 9.1.11 — Delete UnifiedAttackResolver and rewrite tests against action framework
userStories.description:
Tests: unit only — Rewrite existing unified-attack tests to use the action framework pipeline from MFG-51 instead of the standalone resolver.
Context: MFG-51 acceptance criteria required deleting UnifiedAttackResolver.ts and rewriting its tests. This was not done. The action framework (ActionResolution + Action.applyEffects) now handles bead payment, modifier application, and effect execution — making UnifiedAttackResolver redundant.
Current state:
- src/combat/UnifiedAttackResolver.ts still exists (57 lines) — standalone resolveAttack() that duplicates stat composition logic
- features/unit/unified-attack.feature has 7 scenarios testing the standalone resolver directly
- tests/steps/unified-attack.steps.ts (135 lines) instantiates UnifiedAttackResolver directly
- The action framework pipeline (ActionResolution.execute()) now handles weapon stat composition in the effect layer, making the standalone resolver unnecessary
Required changes:
1. Delete src/combat/UnifiedAttackResolver.ts
2. Rewrite features/unit/unified-attack.feature — same 7 scenarios, but test through ActionResolution.execute() with a mock action definition that uses weapon stats
3. Rewrite tests/steps/unified-attack.steps.ts — create Action instances with HydratedEffects, mock BattleAdapter, test via pipeline
4. Fix imports — search for any file importing from UnifiedAttackResolver and redirect to CombatResolver.resolveAttack() or the action pipeline
5. Verify all existing tests pass after deletion (npm run test)
Acceptance Criteria:
- src/combat/UnifiedAttackResolver.ts deleted
- unified-attack.feature tests weapon stat composition through the action pipeline
- All 7 original scenarios have equivalents in the rewritten feature
- No remaining imports of UnifiedAttackResolver anywhere in the codebase
- npm run test passes
